notes: Knockout projections conditioned on the provided completed group stage and the fixed official Round-of-32 bracket.

roundOf32.reasoning: Canada’s group-stage goal difference (+5) and transition pace should punish South Africa’s defensive gaps against higher-tempo sides.
Germany’s +6 group GD and control in settled possession match up well against Paraguay’s more reactive, lower-event profile.
Morocco’s pressing and compactness keep it tight, but the Netherlands’ depth and set-piece threat swing it late.
Brazil’s chance creation and individual duels (7 pts, +6) should overwhelm Japan once the game opens up.
France’s perfect group (9 pts, +8) suggests peak efficiency and defensive control that limits Sweden’s crossing game.
It profiles as a close physical matchup, but Norway’s direct threat and composure in dead-ball moments edges a shootout.
Mexico’s group dominance (9 pts, +6) plus host-region conditions should amplify their pressing and crowd-driven momentum.
England’s structure out of possession and aerial/set-piece edge are well-suited to knockout control against a spirited underdog.
The USA’s athletic pressing and home advantage should tilt the midfield battles and create enough high turnovers to win.
Senegal’s pace makes it even, but Belgium’s experience managing late-game states and marginal finishing edges penalties.
Croatia can slow rhythm and force extra time, yet Portugal’s attacking depth and ball-carrying in wide areas decide it late.
Spain’s +5 GD and ball control should neutralize Austria’s vertical bursts by pinning them into long defending phases.
Switzerland’s organized mid-block and efficient set pieces fit knockout football and limit Algeria’s transition chances.
Argentina’s perfect group (9 pts, +7) indicates a large quality gap that should show in chance volume and finishing.
Colombia’s balance (7 pts, +3) and strong wide defending should withstand Ghana’s athletic surges over 90 minutes.
Egypt’s higher attacking output in the group (5 pts, +2) and ability to counter into space suit this matchup.

roundOf16.reasoning: Germany’s control of central zones and experience in tournament tempo should blunt Canada’s transition-heavy threat.
A tactically tight game turns on Brazil’s individual shot-making and depth in extra time.
France’s defensive speed and duel strength can contain Norway’s direct play while creating higher-quality chances.
Mexico’s home-region edge keeps it level, but England’s set-piece and game-management profile translates in a shootout.
Belgium’s ability to play through pressure and create efficient final-third actions should beat the USA’s press over time.
A low-margin Iberian tie likely hinges on finishing variance, with Portugal shading the shootout.
Switzerland’s structure keeps it close, but Argentina’s creativity and passing between lines generate the decisive moments.
Colombia’s midfield control and ball security should reduce Egypt’s counter volume and produce more sustained pressure.

quarterfinals.reasoning: Germany can match structure-for-structure, but Brazil’s deeper bench and 1v1 advantage decide a tired game in extra time.
France’s transition defense and efficiency in big moments give them the edge in a high-quality, low-error matchup.
Portugal’s ball progression under pressure and late attacking options can turn an even game after 90 minutes.
Argentina’s group-stage form and compact rest-defense should suppress Colombia’s wing transitions and create cleaner looks.

semifinals.reasoning: France’s mix of defensive speed and direct attacking efficiency is well-suited to punishing Brazil’s occasional defensive spacing.
A tactical stalemate feels likely, with Argentina’s nerve and keeper impact giving them the marginal shootout edge.

thirdPlace.reasoning: With slightly more attacking variety and depth, Brazil can capitalize on a more open, lower-pressure third-place game.

final.reasoning: Two elite sides likely trade control, but France’s tournament-level efficiency and late-game physicality tilt extra time.
